issue_title: Fix CI to skip slow tests.
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The slowest tests are marked here:

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/tests/json-fixtures/test_state.py#L62-L113

* These tests **should not** be executed in CI runs initiated by pull requests.
* These tests **should** execute in the daily cron test run.

The test suite uses the `TRAVIS_EVENT_TYPE` to determine if it is running in the `cron` context.

### How can it be fixed

Investigate why this isn't behaving as expected since it looks like the Byzantium test suite is running long when it should be around 5-10 minutes runtime.

### What is wrong?
SMC utils (`SMC (SMCHandler)` and `ShardTracker`) are invoked in Trinity. And currently, there's no error handling in both sides.

#### Exception Types
1. API exceptions
    * Known general exceptions (e.g., `ValueError`)
    * Custom exceptions: 
        * handler: [sharding/exceptions.py at master · ethereum/sharding · GitHub](https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/blob/master/sharding/handler/exceptions.py)
        * `BlockNotFound`: https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/blob/717975aae7361bf738a51365c0f60cf97a594970/sharding/handler/log_handler.py#L9
2. `web3.py` exceptions [web3.py/exceptions.py at master · ethereum/web3.py · GitHub](https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/exceptions.py)
    * If the provider fails to respond, `CannotHandleRequest` will be thrown and web3 will handle it and continue with next provider.
    * If all providers fail to respond, web3 will throw `UnhandledRequest`.
3. Other unexpected exceptions

### How can it be fixed

#### Custom Errors:
1. **[TODO]** Examine will sharding utils throw the right exceptions.
2. Known exceptions:
    * `LogParsingError`: it seems only happening when ABI versions don't match.
    * `BlockNotFound`: [it](https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/blob/717975aae7361bf738a51365c0f60cf97a594970/sharding/handler/log_handler.py#L38-L44) happens when the `from_block` parameter is larger than current block number. "How to set the suitable `from_block`" may depend on the syncing procedure. For now, with the current syncing procedure, the simple solution may be (1) adjusting `from_block` to `current_block_number - current_block_number % self.period_length` and then (2) retry.

#### `web3.py` exceptions:
1. **[TODO]** Examine which exceptions would be raised to `SMC` and how to deal them.
2. If it's retriable
    * For the transaction-calls:
        * Try to make sure whether the transaction is sent or not. (**Q: How to know it?**)
            * If sent, do nothing.
            * If not sent, retry. (**Q: Maximum retries?**)
    * For the polling tasks (e.g., querying `added_header` logs):
        * Retry (**Q: Maximum retries?**)

#### Other unexpected exceptions
Log the error and call `cleanup()`.

### What is wrong?
After `gas_price` is abstracted away and `PAYGAS` is introduced, miner SHOULD(but not mandatory) check if `PAYGAS` is triggered before fully executing a transaction.

### How can it be fixed
Add a `probe_PAYGAS_mode` utility function which executes a transaction with a rather small amount of gas. If miner wants to make sure they will be paid, they should call this function before applying a transaction.

This function computationally normally execute a transaction. Normally this execution terminates with `OutOfGas` error. After the execution terminates, check if `PAYGAS` is triggered and return result.

There is one adjustable system parameter `probe_PAYGAS_mode_gas` that specifies how much gas is provisioned for the execution. Miner should be able to adjust this value depending on how much gas(computation resource) they are willing to risk.

Possible extensions:
- This function returns `True` of `False` indicating if miner will get paid. Additionally we can also return the value of gas price set by `PAYGAS`, to help prioritizing transactions in transaction pool.
- modify `PAYGAS`: If `PAYGAS` is triggered in `probe_PAYGAS_mode`, terminates immediately. This helps saving some computation otherwise it terminates only when gas has run out.
- Should probably check if it terminates in ways that we are expecting, e.g, `OutOfGas` or `PAYGAS` triggered.

### What is wrong?

Right now, we aren't doing anything to validate that the genesis header we download is compatible with the genesis setup used in `trinity`. (not that we support arbitrary genesis settings now, but we will at some point)

### How can it be fixed

See some discussion at: https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/883#discussion_r194305867

Evaluate the genesis file setup we'll use (I think an EIP is in draft or merged on this). Does it fully specify the header? If so, force header creation before launching p2p. If not, save the genesis fields for later validation.

* Version: x.x.x
* Python: 2.7/3.4/3.5
* OS: osx/linux/win

### What was wrong?
Web3 currently supports only named functions

### How can it be fixed?
Add a ```fallback``` arg to ```call``` and ```transact```
```def call(self, transaction=None, fallback=False)```
```def transact(self, transaction=None, fallback=False```
when callings with ```contract.transact({...},fallback=True)```, send the transaction directly

* Version: 4

### What was wrong?

The order of the middleware as passed in to Web3, and treated as part of `add` or `insert`, and returned from `list(w3.middleware_stack)` is inconsistent:

1. in `add` (really, `append`), and `insert()`, the beginning is the bottom of the stack, and the end is the top of the stack
2. in `Web3(..., middlewares=[...])` and `list(w3.middleware_stack)`, the beginning is the top of the stack, and the end is the bottom of the stack

(where the bottom of the stack is the part closest to the node and the top of the stack is the part closest to the Web3 invocation)

This is confusing -- it even caused a bug in #648 .

### How can it be fixed?

~~I think the second order is more natural as part of the implementation, and the first order is more natural in semantic understanding (where you usually want to add new middlewares to the top of the stack, so when you append/add it to the end of the list, that should be the top of the stack).~~

~~Proposal: flip the order in `Web3(middlewares)` and `list(w3.middleware_stack)`.~~

Latest Proposal:
- Switch name from `insert` -> `inject` to avoid any confusion with the list API
- Switch to `inject(middleware, name=, layer=)`, where 0 is the inner-most layer
- Leave the ordering as-is on `Web3(middlewares)` and `list(w3.middleware_stack)`
- Remove all references to first/last/top/bottom/left/right in middleware docs. Always use outer or inner

issue_title: Allow specifying KDF function for `web3.eth.account.encrypt` API
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

`eth-keyfile` supports passing in a `kdf` parameter to specify the key derivation function, but this api is not exposed via the `web3.eth.account.encrypt` API.


### How can it be fixed?

Add an optional keyword parameter `kdf` to allow specifying what KDF you want.

Extra options:

Allow setting the default KDF using both environment variables and via a property on the `web3.eth.account` module.

```python
web3.eth.account.defaultKDF = 'scrypt'
```

issue_body:
I'm trying to get started with conda in Docker.

Creating a new environment with some packages works fine on Debian, but within Docker, it fails in the `LINKING PACKAGE` step when it looks for packages in the read-only directory that contains conda itself:

issue_title: I can't activate environments in centrally administered install
issue_body:
I am getting this message

Cannot activate environment bash.
User does not have write access for conda symlinks.

when I activate an environment created in a shared account. 

Doing something like, from the administrator account,

conda create -n boostenv boost python=2.7

and then from a user account, doing

source activate boostenv

leads to that message. This is a very recent version of conda, 4.1.3 I think. This kind of thing was working with 4.0.3. Are we no longer able to make environments centrally for users, unless they can all write to the central location? That doesn't seem right - note, the filesystems may be different enough between the user home directory and the central location that a hard link is not supported, only soft links will work. I am on linux - rhel7.

  File "C:\Program Files\Anaconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\common\yaml.py", line 26, in yaml_load
    return yaml.load(string, Loader=yaml.RoundTripLoader, version="1.2")
TypeError: load() got an unexpected keyword argument 'version'
```
`version` is not an argument that exists in the conda shipped version of *yaml*, changing the offending line to:

> return yaml.load(string, Loader=yaml.RoundTripLoader)

fixes the issue.

Cheers,

Thomas

issue_title: Updating PyQt4 to PyQt5 leaves an empty PyQt4 directory in site-packages
issue_body:
For some reason, I ended up with an empty 'PyQt4' directory (next to a correct install of PyQt5) in the site-packages directory of an env. 
The reason I noticed this is because it broke the import of pandas (and also of matplotlib): pandas does a `import PyQt4` import to check whether it is installed (which works with an empty directory), and if so, later on imports actual functionality from PyQt4 (which then fails). Somebody else just reported the same problem: https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/16322

I have conda 4.3.17, on Ubuntu 16.04, python 3.5. But, I can't really give more details as I don't remember anymore what I exactly did before this happened. I suppose it was after updating some stuff (eg matplotlib). But, I can not reproduce it in a clean env (first installing pyqt4 and then updating works just fine). 
For @dibgerge the same happend when he updated pandas from 0.19.2 to 0.20.1 (which might also update the matplotlib version, and so pyqt4 version, as pyqt is not a direct dependency of pandas). Maybe he can give more details on what he exactly did.

Sorry for the vague description, but as I was apparently not the only one with this issue, I thought I could report it.

issue_title: [Bug] Called dependency recompiled twice (.cbl then .CBL) but 2nd recomp compiles as .exe and fails.
issue_body:
Cobol module B has linkage so is compiled (successfully) as a module. Cobol module A makes a simple call to module B, passing some params, and is set to compile as an executable. However, compiling module A causes dependency module B to recompile twice - once as a .dll (correctly) and then as a .exe (incorrectly). The log shows the 1st recomp identifying B.cbl but the 2nd shows B.CBL. As it has linkage, the 2nd automatic recomp of the dependency fails.

I just spent several hours going through the code to find out why index selection based on months wasn't working.
For example, if today is April 15, and you want to delete indices that are 2 months older than today e.g. project.2016.02.14 (--prefix project.* --time-unit months --older-than 2 --timestring %Y.%m.%d), curator doesn't delete indices that are dated older than February 15, it deletes indices older than _February 1_.  That is, it goes back to the first day of the current month, _then_ goes back two whole months from that date.  One consequence is that if you have a cron job running curator every day, it won't delete anything for a whole month, then on the first day of the month, it will delete a months worth of indices.  Another consequence is that there is no way to delete indices that are exactly N months older than the current date - you can delete N*30 days ago, or N*4 weeks ago, but that is not the same as N months ago.
Since you can't very well fix `--time-unit months` without breaking existing usage of this counter-intuitive behavior, perhaps introduce another time unit e.g. `exact_months` or something like that.

issue_title: Can't work with China cloud when using connection string
issue_body:
REPO WITH:
```
from azure.storage.blob import BlockBlobService
connection_string = 'A_CONNECTION_STRING_FOR_CHINA_STORAGE_ACCOUNT'
endpoint_suffix = '.core.chinacloudapi.cn'
client = BlockBlobService(connection_string=connection_string, endpoint_suffix=endpoint_suffix)
containers = list(client.list_containers())
print(containers)
```
The script will hang has it attempts to use the endpoint `.blob.core.windows.net` (even though endpoint_suffix is for China cloud) and the storage account at that endpoint does not exist.

Looks like in the `get_service_parameters` method, `endpoint_suffix` is not passed to `_ServiceParameters._from_connection_string` and so the default is used.

Offending file: https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/717f616809c82b6cbf3e258f3d94303e6fabe411/azure/storage/_connection.py#L96

cc: @johanste, @tjprescott, @mayurid

issue_title: Add option to remove trailing cells on save using the content manager
issue_body:
In discussions with @ian-r-rose we realised that many notebooks have an extra cell at the end of the notebook due to execution with the ↲ command, which automatically creates a new cell.

These cells (being empty) have no effect on the output other than to make it look somewhat sloppy when sharing notebooks.

This seems like a common enough use case that it'd be worthwhile having a configurable option that could just create a [presave hook](http://jupyter-notebook.readthedocs.io/en/latest/extending/savehooks.html#examples) that will erase these cells. 

One UI concern that we might have with this is the possibility that this cell is actively selected/being edited at the time that save is invoked, in which case this shouldn't be executed. Other than that though it doesn't seem like this would create many issues and would make for nicer default output for many people. Because it would be an opt-in solution and always keeps a valid notebook file; there would be no compatibility worries. 

NB: I'll work on this later, this was a sidetrack from something else I was doing and it led me down another rabbit hole related to the documentation I link above. I'm just posting this for my further reference.

![same](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36201865/36344279-c928c346-143d-11e8-8d8f-fa8eacd4cdb6.png)

This works:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd.scipy.stats import multivariate_normal as mvn
from autograd import grad, jacobian

mean = np.array([0, 0])
cov = np.array([[1, 0], [0, 100]])
x = np.random.multivariate_normal(mean, cov, 10)
loglik = lambda x:  mvn.logpdf(x, mean=mean, cov=cov); 
grad_loglik = lambda u: to_scalar(jacobian(loglik)(u))
check_grads(grad_loglik, x)
```

issue_title: Regression in v2.0.13: running "coverage combine" overrides existing .coverage
issue_body:
After I run `pytest` with coverage enabled (through `--cov` option),  I have some empty ill-formatted `.coverage.*` files (I will not go into details why, I am not 100% sure exactly, probably this has to do with `Pool.join` not being called in some places).

An example of .coverage* files I have after the `pytest --cov` run (taken from this Travis [log](https://travis-ci.org/lesteve/joblib/jobs/329948292#L2239)):
```
-rw-r--r-- 1 travis travis    0 Jan 17 16:49 .coverage.travis-job-lesteve-joblib-329948292.travisci.net.2842.950710
-rw-r--r-- 1 travis travis    0 Jan 17 16:50 .coverage.travis-job-lesteve-joblib-329948292.travisci.net.3588.085891
-rw-r--r-- 1 travis travis  22K Jan 17 16:52 .coverage
-rw-r--r-- 1 travis travis 2.6K Jan 17 16:52 .coverage.travis-job-lesteve-joblib-329948292.travisci.net.5223.980799
```

Since v2.0.13 (and this [commit](https://github.com/codecov/codecov-python/commit/6354ac225a9413cc19d6418e4829d9376d22ad14#diff-ed4cb86e1f4a5c5feeecc37b90ec6a23)), `coverage combine` is run under the hood by `codecov`. In a setup like mine this will override the existing `.coverage` and result in partial coverage (actually in my case the coverage drop to near zero, because the .coverage.* are very small).

I tried to call `coverage combine -a` before calling `codecov` and that fixes the issue, so you probably want to do that (as well as in the bash tool) unless there is something I am missing.

issue_title: Look into eliminating the fabric_task decorator
issue_body:
_From @hannes-ucsc on April 13, 2015 2:44_

The `@fabric_task` decorator is inconvenient to use. One should be able to call Fabric functions in a box method without having to decorate that method with `@fabric_task`. That decorator's ability to temporarily change the remote user introduces considerable complexity since the decorator needs to maintain the previously set remote user such that it can revert to it when the decorated method returns. The same thing can be achieved with Fabric's `settings` context manager. 

We should be able invoke run(), sudo(), put(), get() by simply setting env.host and env.user. Switching the user should be done using `with settings( user=... )`. 

_Copied from original issue: BD2KGenomics/cgcloud-core#11_

I'm pretty sure this won't impact the code much, except for maybe flagging some non-preemptible jobs.  But the README will have to be changed quite a bit.  From what I'm seeing, we'll probably want to replace all cgcloud based examples with straight toil versions.

issue_title: Why—oh god why—are we passing ints as strings?
issue_body:
While fixing #184, I found that we were calling `str(multiprocessing.cpu_count())`, which I thought was bizarre. It turns out that this is an optimization, because we wind up passing this `cpu_count` to the GATK as a core count, and since that call is going through `docker_call`/`check_call`, etc., it needs to be a string, and so on.

But, upon further reflection here and in #176, it turns out that this is sort of why we're getting OOD errors in the GATK pipelines. Since the cores argument is never set on any of the toil jobs in the GATK germline/preprocessing pipelines, the parameter defaults to 1 core. This leads to the occasional instance where multiple GATKs get put on a single node, which leads to good times, etc. You don't want to blanket set the core count (or disk requirements) because there are various parts of the pipeline that are single threaded, only operate on a reference file, etc.

So, while passing ints as strings is not always wrong, in this case, it was hiding a job resource request bug which led to tons of funny business.

issue_title: Compat for modules like concurrent.futures which are not bulid-in in python2
issue_body:
Hi!
I found `flake8-import-order` can not work well with some modules which are only built-in in python3 in python2 environment, like `concurrent.futures`. For example:
```
$ cat a.py
import concurrent.futures
import click

$ python --version
Python 2.7.10

$ flake8 a.py 
a.py:1:1: F401 'concurrent.futures' imported but unused
a.py:2:1: F401 'click' imported but unused
```
There is no `I100` warning which should show in python2.

Then I modified `a.py`:
```
$ cat a.py 
import click
import concurrent.futures

$ flake8 a.py 
a.py:1:1: F401 'click' imported but unused
a.py:2:1: F401 'concurrent.futures' imported but unused
a.py:2:1: I100 Import statements are in the wrong order.import concurrent.futures should be before import click
```
There is an `I100` warning which should not show in this import order.

I think it should be more flexible in python2 for these modules like `concurrent.futures`.

Thanks for your patience and sorry for my poor English.

issue_title: Explicitly set default assumptions for requests library
issue_body: I _think_ there was a change in a newer version of requests that flipped the default setting of "allow_redirects" from True to False.  Need to make sure our library explicitly sets the things to match our requirements for interacting with BIGIP.

The `fixed_variables` option of `CMAEvolutionStrategy` interacts with `minstd` and `maxstd` and `integer_variables` options. This leads to an error for the former and probably to unexpected results for the latter. We may consider to depreciate `fixed_variables` in favour of `fitness_transformations.FixVariables`. In this case the values for above options would need to be explicitly managed by the user.

issue_title: Don't use `deprecated` decorator on a class
issue_body:
I'm currently on v1.0.2

I recently tried upgrading to 1.1.1 but I encountered a confusing problem.  I started receiving an error like:

```python
TypeError at /
Error when calling the metaclass bases
    function() argument 1 must be code, not str

...

/home/docker/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oscar/apps/catalogue/views.py in <module>
    'catalogue.search_handlers', 'get_product_search_handler_class') ...
▶ Local vars

/home/docker/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oscar/core/loading.py in get_class
    return get_classes(module_label, [classname])[0] ...
▶ Local vars

/home/docker/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oscar/core/loading.py in get_classes
        local_module = _import_module(local_module_label, classnames) ...
▶ Local vars

/home/docker/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/oscar/core/loading.py in _import_module
        return __import__(module_label, fromlist=classnames) ...
▼ Local vars
Variable	Value
classnames	['get_product_search_handler_class']
module_label	'dpm.catalogue.search_handlers'

./dpm/catalogue/search_handlers.py in <module>
    from oscar.apps.catalogue.search_handlers import ProductSearchHandler as OscarProductSearchHandler

    class ProductSearchHandler(OscarProductSearchHandler): ...
        def get_search_queryset(self):
            sqs = super(ProductSearchHandler, self).get_search_queryset()
            return sqs.exclude(num_in_stock=0)
▼ Local vars
Variable	Value
OscarProductSearchHandler	 None
```

It seemed like a circular import or something.  I just now worked out what is wrong ...

In 1.1.1 catalogue/search_handlers.py there is this:

```python
# Deprecated name. TODO: Remove in Oscar 1.2
ProductSearchHandler = deprecated(SolrProductSearchHandler)
```

This is fine if you try and instantiate (i.e. `__call__`) the class. But apparently it breaks any code that tries to inherit from that class.

Once I worked this out I had an easy fix for my app, just import `SolrProductSearchHandler` and inherit from that instead.

...but what happened is not 'deprecation', it was a breaking change - I guess unintentional?

It seems a different decorator is required if 'painless' deprecation of possibly-overridden classes is intended, eg:

```
def deprecated_cls(cls):
    class NewClass(cls):
        def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
            message = "Class '%s' is deprecated and will be " \
                "removed in the next major version of django-oscar" \
                % cls.__name__
            warnings.warn(message, DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)
            super(NewClass, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
    return NewClass
```

issue_title: Infinite Loopback between asp.net core 2.0 controller and login.microsoft.com  inside office addins
Missing creation of StringComparer for other languages
issue_body:
I'm trying to retarget a portable C# library for .Net core 1.0.

I used to do something like this to get a string comparer for a different language, once I obtained a CultureInfo object for the language (via a delegate set by the caller):
IComparer<T> comparer = (IComparer<T>)StringComparer.Create(cultureInfo, caseInsensitive);

.Net Core currently seems to only support it for the current culture via properties like StringComparer.CurrentCulture.

Is there a way to get a string comparer for a given language or culture?

This is important for an app that uses multiple languages simultaneously, such as an app for language-learning.

We should have a new `renew` verb which sets defaults appropriate to renewal, tries to get information from renewal configurations when possible, and actually performs renewals.

The `renew` verb is akin to calling `certonly` for each existing renewal configuration file.

issue_title: GPG key as account signing key (for smartcard/HSM use)
issue_body:
When I register a new user account, I'd like to sign things with my gpg key instead of generating a new keypair. That way, I don't have to worry about keeping secure some new account private key.

Also, this means account private keys could be kept on a smartcard or HSM that's gnupg's configured to use, which means the letsencrypt client doesn't have to roll it's own smartcard/HSM interaction layer.

The goal of this is to allow managed platforms (e.g. shared hosting providers) to deploy an HSM that has their account key, then they can safely issue new HTTPS certificates for their users' websites.

issue_body:
moved from https://github.com/common-workflow-language/common-workflow-language/issues/171
@ntijanic commented on 30 Nov 2015

> Some ordering is lost in conversion, but this could be fixed by setting `@container: @list` in the context for these fields: `engineConfig` and `source`.
> 
> Also, `default` field can in theory include a map or a list. Not sure how to deal with this. Some options:
> - Allow only primitive values for `default` (no maps, lists or files).
> - Require maps and lists to be represented in another way e.g. `class: SerializedValue, value: <JSONString>`.
> - Drop the idea that execution semantics survive loading to RDF graph.
> 
> I suggest we temporarily disallow maps and lists for `default`, and introduce support in future iteration.
> 
>

I am using a fresh install of CKEditor on Django 2.1 and python 3. I'm using it with django-machina, but I don't think that should matter.

To clarify here is exactly what I did:
- pip install'd django-ckeditor
- followed the instructions on this page exactly to edit settings.py: https://django-machina.readthedocs.io/en/stable/customization/recipes/using_another_markup_language.html 

When I tested it out, it allowed me to write Javascript in a forum post by switching to "Source" mode and submitting. The Javascript runs in the post.

I tried to change this by adding the following to `settings.py`: 

```
CKEDITOR_CONFIGS = {
    'default': {
        "removePlugins": "stylesheetparser",
        "allowedContent": False,
    }
};
```

The javascript still ran in the post. I don't know how to disable this. (Is there a way?)

From issue #245 I infer that this is not intended behavior (That issue makes it sound like CKEditor has HTML cleaning by default and thus shouldn't be allowing script tags on a fresh install) If this has actually been changed intentionally, I'd like to argue that this is a security risk and should be changed back to filter it by default. 

Thank you and please let me know if you need more information.

#### Please add a code sample or a nbviewer link, copy-pastable if possible

```import gdal
import numpy as np
import folium

print(folium.__version__)

src = gdal.Open('Pop10.tiff', gdal.GA_Update)
band = src.GetRasterBand(1)
imarray = np.array(band.ReadAsArray())


map= folium.Map(zoom_start=3)

folium.raster_layers.ImageOverlay(
    image=imarray,
    bounds=[[-180, -60], [180, 85]],
    colormap=lambda x: (1, 0, 0, x),
    origin='lower',
).add_to(map)

#Save html
map.save('folium_test.html')

```

When I run this piece of code, i get:

```

  File "fol.py", line 14, in <module>
    folium.raster_layers.ImageOverlay(
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'raster_layers'
```

I've installed folium via pip.

#### Output of ``folium.__version__``
0.5.0

I'm sure I'm doing something wrong here, any ideas?

issue_body: Is there anyway to set an upload bandwidth limit? Right now if I upload a large file, it eats my whole connection bandwidth for several hours which is problematic for other services I'm running. It would be nice to have some option to limit that...

Today, to add a ref to a Schema from a Schema's nested field, we must add a `ref` parameter, as such:

    cat_with_ref = fields.Nested(CategorySchema, ref='Category', description="A category")

To me, it looks like we're repeating `Category` (which is gettable by removing `Schema` from the `CategorySchema` name).

I think we should do it the other way around: automatically add a ref such as `#/definitions/Category` for any `fields.Nested(CategorySchema)`, unless `use_refs=false` is passed to the APISpec.

What do you think ?

issue_title: Refactor: remove MarshmallowPlugin.path_helper, make YAML optional,...
issue_body:
We have two types of plugins:

- web framework plugins:
  - get path from view object (with a [limitation to one path per view](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/flask.py#L107) in Flask, see #181)
  - populate `operations` with info parsed from YAML docstrings

- marshmallow plugin
  - populate `operations` with info parsed from YAML docstrings
  - resolve marshmallow schemas in operations

Several remarks:

The path helper in marshmallow plugin is a generic path helper that expects a view function with a docstring. I'm wondering if it is of any use, since anyone using a web framework would use the dedicated path helper. Assuming a web framework is simple enough not to need more than this raw helper, then it should be `SimpleWebFrameworkPlugin.path_helper` anyway. This is not marshmallow specific at all. I'd be tempted to remove it. (It would break a few tests.) In my framework, I use `MarshmallowPlugin` but I override its path helper with a method that raises `PluginMethodNotImplementedError` to remove that feature.

The `WebFrameworkPlugin`s (flask,...) are more like `YAMLWebFrameworkPlugin`s. I'd be happy to make the YAML requirement optional. I don't use it, as I get all necessary information from the decorators I use to pass serialization and deserialization schemas. I don't think `flask-apispec` uses this either, although I didn't check. If we got rid of `MarshmallowPlugin.path_helper` already, then it's only about importing YAML stuff only in web framework plugins modules.

BTW, `load_yaml_from_docstring` and `load_operations_from_docstring` could be reworked to return `{}` rather than `None` and avoid that `or` test in each plugin: 

```python
    utils.load_operations_from_docstring(view.__doc__) or {}
```

Once we're there, we'll have `MarshmallowPlugin` with only an `operation_helper`, and a few web framework plugins with only a `path_helper`. And only one `path_helper` returning a path string should be registered at a time, as the last one speaking overrides the others. Anyway, only one should be needed.

Also the web framework path helpers deal with operations quite a lot, and perhaps this logic should be split into `path_helper` and `operation_helper`. From a quick glance, the coupling between the two seems loose. Could be a bit trickier with flask due to the `if method in rule.methods` test, which covers what looks like a twisted but legit case (method declared in the view but not in the rule).

If doing this, then the plugin registration order will matter, as those web framework operation helpers will need to be executed before marshmallow helper, for instance. But I don't think this is an issue, apart from the test it breaks. Currently the order does not matter, and there's a test to check that, but it is only because the `operations` work from the web framework plugins is done in the path helper.

Just posting here for feedback before doing anything. There's nothing really blocking in current design AFAIK, so I'm not stuck and I can totally live with it.

issue_title: `dump_only` attributes passed in Meta options are not marked as readOnly
issue_body:
Reported by @lafrech in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/pull/81#issuecomment-233417809:

------

I noticed this only adds readOnly if the field was provided dump_only attribute directly. If the field appears in the dump_only list in Meta, readOnly is not added.

Is this known/intended?

More precisely, it "does not work" if spec.definition is passed a marshmallow.schema.SchemaMeta but it "works" if it is passed a Schema instance:

class GistSchema(ma.Schema):

    content = ma.fields.Str()

    class Meta:

# readOnly not added

# readOnly added
spec.definition('Gist', schema=GistSchema())
I just begun experimenting with apispec and I'm not sure passing an instance is the right thing to do. In the docs, a SchemaMeta is passed.

issue_title: Create a menu bar for the web app / serverextension
issue_body:
Time to take apart what we support / feature for a menu bar. Generally we should have feature parity between the Jupyter Notebook menu bar (with some deletions) and the nteract desktop app. Here's what I think our MVP needs:

* File
  * New Notebook...
    * Kernel1
    * Kernel2 (these are generated by a call to the `/api/kernelspecs` endpoint)
  * Open
  * Save
  * Download As...
* Edit
  * Cut Cell
  * Copy Cell
  * Paste Cell Above
  * Paste Cell Below
  * Delete Cell
  * Move Cell Up
  * Move Cell Down
* Cell
  * Run All
  * Run All Below
  * Insert Cell Above
  * Insert Cell Below
  * Clear All Outputs
  * Unhide All Outputs
  * Unhide All Inputs
* Runtime (as opposed to Language, fitting with colab's choice)
  * Interrupt
  * Kill
  * Restart
  * Reconnect
  * Switch to...
    * Kernel 1
    * Kernel2 (these are generated by a call to the `/api/kernelspecs` endpoint)
* Help
  * Learn more
  * Release Notes (vX.Y.Z)

If there are mismatches to what I have above and what you find in the various frontends, lets figure out what we think our opinionated consistent "right" way to do this will be.

/cc @klebba

issue_title: replace old style print-s in the examples
issue_body:
Hi,
Great stuff, however the examples are full of old style print-s ("print whatever" instead of "print(whatever)"). These do not work with recent python 3 (plus having used other programming languages these are plain ugly and WTF-ish (not sure why python introduced the old-style print in the first place)). Plus these confuse PyCharm formatting - should you try to copy-paste such code to PyCharm.

I think **logging.info() should be the best replacement for print-s**. Initialization of logging is easy with logging.basicConfig(), and **it would also make it easy for the users to change the log level to DEBUG to see ncclient debug logs if needed while playing with the examples**.

I would do a PR for this if needed, and if my proposal is acceptable by the author(s) and/or maintainer(s).

Example for basicConfig:
```
import logging

issue_title: Add satellite zenith angle filter for geostationary imagery
issue_body:
Certain users (US National Weather Service Forecasters) would like geostationary full disk or mesoscale images (GOES-16+ and H8+) to be masked where satellite zenith angle is greater than a certain amount (maybe >83). Points beyond this threshold should be masked and be treated the same as space pixels would in the final output.

This is a desired feature because on certain visualization tools (AWIPS, etc) the image may be viewed in multiple projections and a pixel near the edge of the earth in the geostationary projection gets very distorted in other projections ("spiky"). The easiest example is a full disk GOES-16 image in a polar-stereographic projection near Alaska. The forecasters would rather have no data near the outer edge of the disk than have the huge distorted spikes they are seeing.

The question is how does this get applied in SatPy? Right now we don't have a defined interface for this type of functionality except for maybe a modifier. I think in the past @mraspaud and I had talked about a filter versus a modifier, where a filter is just a modifier that modifies the data in place. Maybe inplace writes aren't something we want. This feature will also be desired for GOES and Himawari satellite imagery so it doesn't make a ton of sense to add it to a specific reader. The only issue I have making it a modifier is that it will make it difficult for the user to request it; that is unless `scn.load([...], modifiers=('satellite_zenith_filter',))` is possible.

@kylebystrom can you help clean up some of the sample dataframes?

* Don't need an "idx" column (there is already a material_id column that serves as an index)
* Actually make the "material_id" the index column of the dataframe - see for example the ``index_col`` parameter of ``pd.read_csv`` (you'll notice there is no extraneous index column after doing this).
* if ``volume`` is volume per site, rename to ``volume_per_site``. If it's not per site, probably remove it.
* reorder the columns to be in the following order

1. The index column ("material_id")
2. The formula/composition
3. Other parameters that succintly describe the input structure (nsites, volume, space_group, etc)
4. The actual structure column itself
5. Simple (float) output data - band gap, K_VRH, etc.
6. Complex (vector, tensor) output data - elastic tensor, piezoelectric tensor, etc.
7. Any extra metadata (cif, poscar, etc) that is essentially redundant info with structure

Because of the column dependence of FunctionFeaturizer (which we solved previously by making feature_labels() None and postprocessing the dataframe in featurize_dataframe), it doesn't play nicely with MultipleFeaturizer.  This seems not ideal, since I'd imagine function featurizer would often be used in a multiplefeaturizer context, but we're going to need to revisit how to flexibly handle feature_labels when feature_labels depend on input columns if we want to fix it.

Test to  copy tag in docker plugin copies the pulp)user_metadata

We currently default to True for backward compatibility reasons, but I would venture to guess that vast majority of users do not care about the IDs of the tasks that their emitted tuples went to.  Disabling is a nice free speedup, but if we made it disabled by default, we would be cutting out extra work for most users.

issue_title: [tenkai] Improve output in case of errors
issue_body:
While tenkai shows the state of a deployment (success and fail) it does not show any further information to help debugging in the case of a failed deployment.

So I suggest three improvements here, that we then maybe split up into separate issues when needed.

## Show Instances
CodeDeploy shows in the UI for Deployments the state of each instances that was deployed to. A Short overview with Information like
* Instance ID
* Status
* Most recent event
* (if there are errors): output (see next point)
at the end of the deployment would be really helpful to find out which instances failed.
![bildschirmfoto 2017-06-29 um 15 36 01](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2162832/27690434-9d05d6e6-5ce1-11e7-986f-317daf0314d4.png)

## Show Failed Events Logs
In case of a failure during on of the CodeDeploy Agents steps it is possible via the AWS UI to get a log of stdout of the failed steps.
This log should be shown per instances in the view described above
![bildschirmfoto 2017-06-29 um 15 38 01](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2162832/27690433-9d04b18a-5ce1-11e7-9fe7-d160417bd203.png)

## Show CloudWatch Logs
This is something that is a bit specific to our glomex setup but still may be useful for others.
Our Instances send the contents of `/var/log/messages` to CloudWatch logs to a log group named `/var/log/messages` where the stream name is then the instance id.

It would be great to have that as optional output (via a parameter) in case of a failure for further debugging.

issue_title: C++ object is destructed before it can be used, when returned as a `shared_ptr` and using default holder type
``shared_ptr`` holder constructed but not freed by pybind11
issue_body:
I think I have found a problem with the construction/destruction of smart pointer holders. It seems like pybind11 does not always destruct the smart pointer holders it creates, because it thinks it doesn't "own" them.

Here is a small code snippet that demonstrates that behavior. It registers a class that uses ``std::shared_ptr`` as holder type. As suggested by the pybind11 documentation, ``std::enable_shared_from_this`` is used so that shared pointers and raw pointers can be used interchangeably.

```cpp
	class MyClass : public std::enable_shared_from_this<MyClass> {};
	py::class_<MyClass, std::shared_ptr<MyClass>>(m, "MyClass");

	std::shared_ptr<MyClass> instance = std::make_shared<MyClass>();	
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "1", that's the shared_ptr instance above.

	py::object o = py::cast(instance.get());  // Note we are passing a raw pointer to cast()
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "2", we now have a 2nd shared_ptr (the holder)

	o = py::none();	// Delete Python variable
	std::cout << instance.use_count() << std::endl;	// Prints "2" (This seems wrong! The holder should have been destroyed)
```

Note that ``cast()`` assumes ``return_value_policy::reference``, because we are passing it a raw pointer. Even though a new ``shared_ptr`` holder is constructed by pybind11 as part of the cast, the ``wrapper->owned`` field is set to ``false`` by ``type_caster_generic::cast()``. This prevents the internal ``shared_ptr`` from getting freed when the wrapper is destroyed. A dangling reference to the C++ class instance remains.

The above problem can be worked around either by passing the ``shared_ptr`` to ``cast()``, not the raw pointer. Or by explicitly passing ``return_value_policy::take_ownership`` to ``cast()`` as second argument.

However, my C++ interface that I am trying to wrap with pybind11 typically returns raw pointers and relies on the ``std::enable_shared_from_this`` mechanism. This allows constructing reference counted pointers from raw pointers only when needed. In other words, I need ``cast()`` to behave the same for shared pointers and raw pointers. In both cases a ``shared_ptr`` holder should be constructed and, when the Python wrapper goes out of scope, destructed again.

I try to run some of the tests. Some of them start with:

```
import pytest

with pytest.suppress(ImportError):
    import numpy as np
```

Leads to:

```
/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/bin/python3.5 /pybind11/tests/test_numpy_array.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/pybind11/tests/test_numpy_array.py", line 3, in <module>
    with pytest.suppress(ImportError):
AttributeError: module 'pytest' has no attribute 'suppress'
```

Seems that `pytest` does not have that attribute `suppress`.

I think I have a version of `pytest` which is not compatible with `pybind11`. Which version am I supposed to use?

Seems that I have installed 3.0.3 by "Holger Krekel, Bruno Oliveira, Ronny Pfannschmidt, Floris Bruynooghe, Brianna Laugher, Florian Bruhin and others"

issue_title: Why 2 datalab version? one in root, the another in google folder.
issue_body:
The two version of datalab are quite different.

More specifically, the execute function in pydatalab/datalab/bigquery/_query.py line 496  is 
def execute(self, table_name=None, table_mode='create', use_cache=True, priority='interactive',
while the execute function in pydatalab/google/datalab/bigquery/_query.py line 322 is
def execute(self, output_options=None, sampling=None, context=None, query_params=None):

so, I can output query result to bigquery table with the pydatalab/datalab/bigquery/ with
import datalab.bigquery as bq
bq.Query(query).execute(table_name=name)

while with pydatalab/google/datalab/bigquery/_query.py
import google.datalab.bigquery as bq
bq.Query(query).execute(output_options=bq.QueryOutput.table(name=name))

It is confusing to have two versions, and no explanation about that on Readme.
Thanks!

issue_title: add getter methods to the RaidenAPI
issue_body:
this is a split-up from #10, as stated in #325 

This are mainly getter methods for a Client to retrieve the initial state of the node on the first connect. Ideally, every following state-update will be retrieved through events in a long-polling fashion, in order to keep the polling count low.

For now, the methods should only return internal Objects like e.g. a `raiden.channel.Channel`.
But in the end, we want the same Interface for external api-users (via TCP) and internal api-users (in-process). Therefore, the API-methods should return Objects that function as simple data-containers for all the relevant information in their decoded (binary) form.
Internal users of the API will simply process this objects, without worrying about encoding,
while external users will retrieve the JSON-encoded version of this objects.

**add following methods to the `raiden-service.RaidenAPI`**:

- `get_channel_list(asset_address=None, partner_address=None)`:
This is a universal method for retrieving a collection of channels.
When neither of the keyword-arguments is provided, it should return all channel-objects that the node participates in. The keyword-arguments serve as a simple filter, so that the client can retrieve channels filtered by a certain asset- / partner address.
e.g. RESTful: `/api/assets/<asset_address>/channels GET`
Returns a list of channels

- `get_channel(channel_address)`:
Returns only one channel-object, retrieved by the netting-channel-address.
This is not neccessarily needed, since we can retrieve all channels with `get_channel_list`,
but is a convienience method if the client is only interested in one channel, or needs to poll for state changes

- `query_endpoint(raiden_address)`:
Querys raiden's discovery to get the value of the `address - > endpoint` mapping.
Returns an endpoint-object (with fields: `address`, `port`)
This will serve for light-clients to establish a connection to a node without having to know the discovery.

issue_title: Path expand bug and signature issues
issue_body:
Here is an MWE:

```
[global]
cwd = path('~/tmp')
wasp_dir = f'{cwd:a}/jnbinder-master'

[default: provides = f"{wasp_dir}/README.md"]
output: f"{wasp_dir}/README.md"
download: decompress = True, dest_file = f'{cwd:a}/WASP.zip'
    https://github.com/vatlab/jnbinder/archive/master.zip
bash: expand = True
    rm -f {cwd}/WASP.zip
```

Running this script multiple times will fully execute the workflow even though the output does exist:

```
$ sos run test.sos 
INFO: Executing default_0: 
INFO: input:   []
WASP.zip
INFO: output:   [file_target('//home/gaow/tmp/jnbinder-master/README.md')]                                     
INFO: Workflow default (ID=cfd8a518d023dd40) is executed successfully.
$ sos run test.sos 
INFO: Executing default_0: 
INFO: input:   []
WASP.zip
INFO: output:   [file_target('//home/gaow/tmp/jnbinder-master/README.md')]                                     
INFO: Workflow default (ID=cfd8a518d023dd40) is executed successfully.
```

However when I replace `cwd = path('~/tmp')` with `cwd = path('/home/gaow/tmp')` I get this at the 2nd run:

```
$ sos run test.sos 
INFO: Executing default_0: 
INFO: input:   []
INFO: default_0 (index=0) is ignored due to saved signature
INFO: output:   [file_target('/home/gaow/tmp/jnbinder-master/README.md')]
INFO: Workflow default (ID=de6478e2d9728cca) is executed successfully.
```

that is, the existing file is properly skipped. Note that for the first case the file target starts with `//home/gaow` (double `//`). I guess this is what caused the trouble.

I'm just showing one example here but actually this bug impacts many of my existing workflows -- they now no longer automatically skip completed steps.

```

issue_title: UserMixin.expiration_timedelta() returns wrong value because timedelta is calculated using different timezones
issue_body:
I am using google oauth2 with django. The UserMixin provides expiration check by access_token_expired() that executes expiration_timedelta() inside.
So, the problem is in expiration_timedelta. Code of the method:
```python
        if self.extra_data and 'expires' in self.extra_data:
            try:
                expires = int(self.extra_data.get('expires'))
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                return None

            now = datetime.utcnow()

            # Detect if expires is a timestamp
            if expires > time.mktime(now.timetuple()):
                # expires is a datetime, return the remaining difference
                return datetime.fromtimestamp(expires) - now
            else:
                # expires is the time to live seconds since creation,
                # check against auth_time if present, otherwise return
                # the value
                auth_time = self.extra_data.get('auth_time')
                if auth_time:
                    reference = datetime.fromtimestamp(auth_time)
                    return (reference + timedelta(seconds=expires)) - now
                else:
                    return timedelta(seconds=expires)

```

The problem occurs because we use UTC now time for current moment, but we use LOCAL time when executing `datetime.fromtimestamp`.
How it works?
Imagine that I am in +2 timezone and right now I have a 12 o'clock UTC and expiration time is 3600 seconds. I got access_token and refresh_token at 11 o'clock UTC, so, my token is already expired. But, when I get auth_time then I'll get 13 o'clock LOCAL + expiration time = 14 o'clock. Timedelta is 14 o'clock LOCAL subtract 12 o'clock UTC = 2 hours(It's strange, but python calculates it like these datetimes at the same timezone). And then we check expiration_timedelta().total_seconds() <= 0 and we got false instead of true.

The fix is very simple: use `datetime.utcfromtimestamp` instead of `datetime.fromtimestamp`.
I do that expiration check manually in my project right now and all works smoothly.

I'll provide a pull request with fix.

issue_title: Clarify documentation on app input resolution
issue_body:
[Here](http://parsl.readthedocs.io/en/latest/userguide/apps.html#special-keywords-arguments) we say:
> inputs : (list) This keyword argument allows you to pass a list of input Futures, and thus wait on the results of these futures to be resolved before execution.

We should clarify this to explain that `inputs` can be used in case you want to add an arbitrary number of dependencies at call time. It is implied in our documentation that ONLY futures in `inputs` will be resolved, but really, any argument or keyword argument that is a future will be resolved.

issue_title: root span does not reach collector when running under uwsgi
issue_body:
If I run my app using werkzeug.serving.run_simple all spans are propagated just fine and my traces/spans are error free. If I run the app under uwsgi and initialize the tracer in the uwsgi.post_fork_hook, i see the client claiming that it has reported the root span, but the collector never appears to receive it (I added logging when the collector receives spans). Once the root span is missing all the other spans that were one level below the root are marked as having an invalid parent, and the trace is screwed up. 

Is there some consideration I'm missing when running jaeger in a tornado.wsgi.WSGIApplication under uwsgi? I suspect it may be an issue with my hacked together middleware that maintains a stack of the ongoing trace in thread-local storage ala py_zipkin. I understand how putting the trace context in TLS could be a problem in a multithreaded environment with concurrent requests, but this issue appears even when I'm only sending in a single request at a time as a proof of concept.

Or am I missing some subtlety related to how tracer flushes as discussed in #50 ?

Comment 1 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
Good catch. The PrettyXMLSerializer code is explicit about this (mis)behaviour:

if isinstance(subject, BNode):
    def subj_as_obj_more_than(ceil):
        return more_than(store.triples((None, None, subject)), ceil)
    #here we only include BNode labels if they are referenced
    #more than once (this reduces the use of redundant BNode identifiers)
    if subj_as_obj_more_than(1):
        writer.attribute(RDF.nodeID, fix(subject))

and this produces exactly the disconnect that you are seeing.

Just to confirm: this issue is restricted to the output of PrettyXMLSerializer - choose any serialization other than pretty-xml and you will be able to parse it back into a properly-connected graph.

IMO, it's a false economy and could usefully be dispensed with.

Fixed in changeset 3ddb26ab8f4f - check for BNode references > 1 commented out.
